retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Once a shoulder dystocia is identified, it is important for the delivering clinician to clearly inform the other team members so they can immediately call for additional support (eg, neonatal and anesthesia clinicians), assist with any maneuvers, and begin proper documentation. The delivering clinician can then attempt specified obstetric maneuvers in a sequential order to relieve the shoulder dystocia. Maneuvers are typically divided into first-line, second-line, and heroic maneuvers. [2] [18] Fundal pressure should be avoided during shoulder dystocia maneuvers. Not only does it not help with the delivery of the fetal shoulders, it may worsen the shoulder impaction and increase the risk of uterine rupture. [1] Furthermore, patients should be instructed to stop pushing while the maneuvers are being performed. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency requiring preparation and training for proper management by the delivering clinician and other obstetric team members. Not only does the infant need to be delivered quickly, but healthcare professionals must also use a careful and calm approach to mitigate the risk of injury to the parturient and the neonate. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Treatment / Management -- Documentation of Shoulder Dystocia
retrieved_docs.content: Documentation by a member of the obstetrical team of delivery times and sequence of events is vital not only to the delivering clinician but also to the patient and their future healthcare practitioners. Furthermore, following the delivery, proper documentation assists clinicians in reviewing what occurred during the delivery and counseling patients on subsequent pregnancy risks. It also provides any future obstetrical clinicians involved in that patient's care insight into the delivery events. Clinical information important to post-delivery documentation includes prepregnancy maternal weight, total maternal weight gain, estimated fetal weight, duration of active labor, duration of the second stage, and delivery time of the fetal head and body. [1] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Documentation by a member of the obstetrical team of delivery times and sequence of events is vital not only to the delivering clinician but also to the patient and their future healthcare practitioners. Furthermore, following the delivery, proper documentation assists clinicians in reviewing what occurred during the delivery and counseling patients on subsequent pregnancy risks. It also provides any future obstetrical clinicians involved in that patient's care insight into the delivery events. [1] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the relevant anatomy, physiology, and risk factors of shoulder dystocia. Effectively implement the proper sequence of obstetric maneuvers recommended in managing a shoulder dystocia. Employ appropriate evaluation and management strategies for patients with shoulder dystocia injuries. Delineate how to effectively collaborate and communicate among the interprofessional team when shoulder dystocia occurs to optimize outcomes for the parturient and infant. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: ACOG recommends developing an obstetric rapid response team and protocols that can be instituted based on clinical triggers. [25] [30] This team should include obstetric clinicians and nurses, anesthesia personnel, and neonatal team members who will care for the infant immediately after delivery. Furthermore, having anesthesia clinicians immediately available in case initial maneuvers fail and the presence of neonatal team members in the room at delivery can be critical in providing optimal infant resuscitation if needed. Hospital guidelines should also identify and address the need for additional personnel (eg, operating room technicians, blood bank and laboratory staff, and page-system operators). [25] Suspected shoulder dystocia is an appropriate indication to summon a rapid response team if the dystocia is not resolved rapidly.

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Routine episiotomy : Although some experts have recommended wide episiotomies be used before performing second-line maneuvers in all patients to gain more access, ACOG and the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (RCOG) both recommend against universally performing an episiotomy in shoulder dystocia cases. This is because shoulder dystocia is, fundamentally, a boney obstruction, and incising the soft tissue of the perineum does not relieve this obstruction. ACOG and RCOG recommend considering an episiotomy only if the delivering clinician believes it will help them perform the second-line maneuvers better. [1] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency; most are unpredictable and unavoidable. The primary objective of any recommended management protocol is the prevention of neonatal asphyxia and brain injury. Therefore, any clinician involved with a vaginal delivery must have the clinical knowledge to promptly identify shoulder dystocia and be prepared to perform any additional obstetric maneuvers required to effect delivery. Competency in the recommended sequence of maneuvers and coordinated management with interprofessional clinicians can prevent permanent neonatal injury and asphyxiation when shoulder dystocia occurs. However, clinicians deficient in this clinical knowledge may become a mechanism of greater maternal and neonatal injury by attempting to resolve shoulder dystocia inappropriately. Consequently, healthcare professionals need enhanced competence when managing shoulder dystocia, updated knowledge, skills, and strategies to identify this complication, effectively intervene, and coordinate care. This will improve patient outcomes and reduce maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effectively performing maneuvers to manage shoulder dystocia is a team effort. Management algorithms should be practiced regularly with simulation training by all personnel that may be present in a delivery room, including physicians, midwives, nurses, technicians, and trainees. [30] [25] [31] This training should focus on correctly performing maneuvers, including heroic ones, and practicing clear, standardized communication between team members. Regular practice in managing emergencies through simulations can help ensure that all obstetric healthcare team members are adequately trained in their respective roles when performing shoulder dystocia maneuvers. Participation in emergency drills can identify clinical errors frequently made during emergencies, increase effective communication between healthcare team members, and reinforce proper protocol. Several studies have shown that simulation training also reduces maternal and neonatal morbidity. [1] [25] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Management includes calling for help, stopping maternal pushing, first-line McRoberts maneuver with or without suprapubic pressure, followed by second-line internal maneuvers.

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, teams should always discuss the potential for shoulder dystocia in patients with risk factors before delivery. Advanced consideration and planning will allow team members to be mentally and physically prepared to act quickly as needed. The delivering clinician should assess the fetal position during pushing and clearly communicate before delivery of the fetal head which direction suprapubic pressure should be applied in case shoulder dystocia becomes apparent. [1] [25] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal complications include third- or fourth-degree tears or postpartum hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Zavanelli maneuver: The fetal head is rotated to the occiput anterior position, flexed, and pushed back up the vagina and into the uterus, effectively reversing the cardinal movements of labor and relieving the compression on the umbilical cord. This fetus is held in place until emergent cesarean delivery is performed. Abdominal rescue: If a cephalic replacement (ie, the Zavanelli maneuver) is unsuccessful, a low transverse hysterotomy is performed, and the fetal shoulder is dislodged and manually rotated through the transabdominal incision, followed by vaginal delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Etiology -- Risk Factors for Shoulder Dystocia
retrieved_docs.content: Intrapartum conditions that may signal the possibility of impending shoulder dystocia include a prolonged second stage of labor, failure of the fetal head to descend, and need for the operative (ie, forceps or vacuum) vaginal delivery of the fetal head. [2] However, while it is essential to remain mindful of the risk factors for shoulder dystocia to prepare for delivery properly, attempts to predict shoulder dystocia based on risk factors have shown poor reliability and have a poor predictive value. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of recurrent shoulder dystocia is estimated at ≥10%. [1] [26] A comprehensive analysis of risk factors and the overall clinical picture should be reviewed and discussed with the patient in subsequent pregnancies. The risks and benefits of cesarean delivery should also be discussed with the patient since this is the only way to prevent recurrent shoulder dystocia confidently. According to ACOG, cesarean delivery should not be universally recommended to all patients with previous shoulder dystocia as it often does not reoccur; rather, decisions regarding the mode of delivery in future pregnancies should be individualized based on clinical factors and the patient's preferences. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Shoulder Dystocia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: When performing the Zavanelli maneuver, inhalational anesthetics or nitroglycerin may help to relax the uterus, although this is not well supported.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: indication and to fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction, and is linked to “Unspecified malposition or malpresentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Collectively, the network connects congenital and acquired uterine‑cervical anomalies, prior cesarean‑section scars, major skeletal pelvic abnormalities, and a spectrum of obstetric complications (labor failure, postpartum hemorrhage, cervical laceration, shoulder dystocia, fetal death) to specific maternal‑care indications. Recognizing these hierarchical and relational links enables comprehensive risk assessment, targeted monitoring, and appropriate intervention strategies in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: variables, especially multiple‑fetus pregnancies (twin, triplet, quadruplet حمل متعدد), increase uterine volume, the likelihood of malpresentation, preterm labor, prolonged labor (مخاض طويل), induced labor (مخاض محفز), and the need for specialized obstetric management. Cervical‑related difficult delivery (عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم), uterine atony (ونى الرحم), retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage (انحباس المشيمة أو الأغشية، من دون نزف), postpartum sepsis (إنتان الدم بعد الولادة), malignant placental tumor (ورم خبيث في المشيمة), and other delivery complications are also captured within the same SMQ. Management strategies depend on the specific presentation and degree of disproportion. Cephalic disproportion may be addressed by external cephalic version, operative vaginal delivery, or cesarean section. Shoulder dystocia and other obstructive presentations require prompt obstetric interventions, while normal pregnancy and delivery—spontaneous vaginal birth, natural birth

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: disturbances, fetal‑pelvic size mismatches, pelvic skeletal malformations, and specific malpositions converge to produce a spectrum of labor complications, postpartum hemorrhage, shoulder dystocia, and potential fetal death, guiding clinicians in diagnosis, classification, and management of obstetric patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 621
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal Positions and Presentations includes the condition عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض, which denotes a mismatch between the fetal head size and the maternal pelvis. Fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction also has the child عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض and is classified as عُسْرُ وِلادَةِ (الحِزامِ) الكَتِفِيّ، مع الولادة, indicating a shoulder‑dystocia type difficulty during delivery. High risk pregnancies are clinically associated with عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض as well as نزيف مهبلي, highlighting that cephalopelvic disproportion contributes to obstetric complications such as obstructed labor, shoulder dystocia, and vaginal bleeding. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for risk assessment, monitoring, and management of patients at high obstetric risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: غير محدد، قبل الولادة) and is connected to “Unspecified disproportion, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” both of which fall under the broader “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” These hierarchical relationships illustrate how congenital and acquired cervical or uterine anomalies, scar‑related pelvic pathology, and prior cesarean history contribute to obstetric complications such as cervical incompetence, fetopelvic disproportion, shoulder dystocia, postpartum hemorrhage, and fetal death (موت الجنين), each serving as specific indications for targeted maternal care and organized within a structured clinical knowledge network.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are recognized as a form of failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض) and are also classified as causes of a prolonged first stage of labor (مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض) as well as a prolonged second stage of labor (مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض). These classifications reflect that abnormal fetal positioning, size mismatch, or other intrapartum problems can impede the initiation of effective uterine contractions and can extend both early and later phases of the birthing process, highlighting the need for careful monitoring and appropriate obstetric interventions to prevent labor dystocia and improve maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 148

Shoulder dystocia and other obstructive presentations require prompt obstetric interventions, while normal pregnancy and delivery—spontaneous vaginal birth, natural birth (přirozený porod, natural birth (přirozený porod)) and routine postpartum assessment (Zorg en onderzoek onmiddellijk na bevalling)—are categorized under “other pregnancy and delivery including normal” and serve as the reference for individualized perinatal care. The integrated framework links fetal presentation, maternal pelvic anatomy, gestational factors, and labor physiology (porodní děj, reproductive system physiological phenomena) to guide clinical assessment, risk stratification, and optimal maternal‑neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and also as ورم دموي توليدي حوضي. This group includes postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), obstetric cervical laceration (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). Fetopelvic disproportion/obstruction (عُسْرُ وِلادَةِ (الحِزامِ) الكَتِفِيّ، مع الولادة, shoulder dystocia) is a child of “Major abnormality of bony pelvis” and of “significantly abnormal pelvis, unspecified, pre‑delivery” (حوض عظمي غير طبيعي بشكل كبير، غير محدد، قبل الولادة). Unspecified disproportion (Unspecified disproportion, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable) has broader relationships to both the unspecified labor‑and‑delivery indication and to fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction, and is linked to “Unspecified malposition or malpresentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Collectively, the network

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2339
retrieved_docs.content: Fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction is classified as عُسْرُ وِلادَةِ (الحِزامِ) الكَتِفِيّ، مع الولادة, indicating that this condition corresponds to a shoulder dystocia situation during delivery in which the fetal shoulders become trapped in the maternal pelvis, resulting in obstructed labor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 67
retrieved_docs.content: further influencing delivery dynamics and increasing the likelihood of cesarean section. Placental pathology, exemplified by intervillous hemorrhage of the placenta, is classified as a disease of the membranes or amniotic fluid and contributes to adverse perinatal outcomes. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures related to the genital and breast regions, such as the use of a vaginal speculum for imaging, support the evaluation and management of the above conditions. All of these entities are hierarchically linked to the normal obstetric processes of normal pregnancy, spontaneous vaginal delivery, and normal delivery procedure. Recognizing each specific condition—whether a cervical polyp, rigid cervix, breech presentation, shoulder dystocia, fetal hydrops, or pelvic abnormality—enables clinicians to anticipate delivery challenges, select appropriate interventions (e.g., cervical cerclage, conversion of breech to cephalic, cesarean section), and optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: والحوض (fetal‑pelvic disproportion) and classified as عُسْرُ وِلادَةِ (الحِزامِ) الكَتِفِيّ، مع الولادة (shoulder‑belt dystocia). Fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction also classifies itself as a distinct obstetric obstruction entity, and the child entity عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض appears under Fetal Positions and Presentations, linking fetal presentation issues to pelvic size mismatches. “Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother” is a parent concept that includes نزف ما بعد الولادة (post‑partum hemorrhage) as a child and is classified as تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (birth‑related cervical tear). This same parent category also classifies “Other and unspecified abnormality of organs and soft tissues of pelvis, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” illustrating the intersection of postpartum complications with general pelvic soft‑tissue abnormalities. The concept of a previous cesarean section (عملية قيصرية سابقة) is classified under maternal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: unspecified as to episode of care, thereby linking pelvic abnormalities to adverse maternal outcomes. Pregnancy observable interprets إجهاض معتاد (regular miscarriage) and is influenced by Uterine scar from previous surgery affecting pregnancy, illustrating that a uterine scar (previous cesarean section) shapes the clinical assessment of pregnancy. Overall, pelvic organ and soft‑tissue abnormalities—particularly uterine scars from prior cesarean sections, cervical incompetence, and major bony pelvis anomalies—are central determinants of obstetric complications such as fetopelvic disproportion, shoulder dystocia, cervical lacerations, fetal death, and the need for specific maternal‑care indications. Recognizing these hierarchical and classificatory relationships supports risk stratification, counseling, and management of pregnant patients with a history of pelvic surgery or congenital pelvic abnormalities.

Collectively, the ontology links umbilical cord complications, multiple gestations, labor dystocia, pelvic anatomical abnormalities, fetopelvic disproportion, shoulder dystocia, perineal trauma, assisted vaginal delivery, and a spectrum of postpartum adverse events under a unified SMQ framework, enabling clinicians to identify interconnected risk factors, anticipate associated injuries, and plan targeted interventions to improve maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: In obstetric practice, the overarching topic “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” includes the specific condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which is classified as a subcategory (has parent) within these care indications. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: (triplet pregnancy delivered), and “حمل بأربعة توائم، غير محدد لنوبة الرعاية” (quadruplet pregnancy unspecified care)—are children of “multiple pregnancy” (حمل متعدد) and are also SMQ members, highlighting their status as risk factors for obstetric complications. Labor‑related difficulties incorporated in the SMQ include cervical dystocia (“عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم”), prolonged labor (“مخاض طويل”), induced labor (“مخاض محفز”), failure to induce labor (“فشل تحريض المخاض”), and unspecified fetal‑pelvic disproportion/obstruction, which are linked through the broader “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery.” Pelvic structural abnormalities—such as major bony pelvis malformation (“حوض عظمي غير طبيعي بشكل كبير، غير محدد، قبل الولادة”) and unspecified maternal pelvic deviation—serve as parent concepts for specific obstetric obstruction entities like “fetopelvic disproportion; obstruction” and the associated shoulder dystocia (“عُسْرُ وِلادَةِ
